speaker: interviewer
text: This is tape number nine. Bruce Cohen interviewing my wonderful friend, Halina Nelken. 24th of November, 1995. And as we continue from the last tape, Halina, you had just indicated that you had arrived in the United States in 1959 with a son and you-- with a son and your mother. Is that correct?

speaker: interviewee
text: Right.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what city did you arrive in?

speaker: interviewee
text: We arrived in New York. And in addition to my son and my mother, I had my two diplomas, one from the University of Krakow, which is only 300 years older than Harvard University. It has two famous alumnus-- alumna, one, Nicolaus Copernicus and another one, myself. And so, we-- I got my degree in history of art and philosophy. And still as a student, I began my scholarly work at the National Museum in Krakow. And fairly early, I began to publish papers-- scholarly papers-- and books. Before I left Poland, I published two books, which each one had two different editions in reprints. One was on a painter, Wyspianski, another one on painter Jan Matejko. And from New York, I went to [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Texas, Fort Worth. And there I was in Fort Worth Art Center as a-- working as a painting restorer.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what about the-- any other faculty positions you'd want to talk about?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, from Fort Worth, I came to New York. And then from New York, Professor Doctor Jakob Rosenberg from Fogg Art Museum took me as an assistant to him at the Fogg Art Museum in 1961. There I was till about six years, I was working at the Fogg. And then later on, I began to teach, doing still research and writing books. I was teaching in different colleges around here. I was still doing research for Fogg Art Museum. I organized a very large bicentennial exhibition on Alexander von Humboldt, for which I got an award from Alexander von Humboldt Foundation in Bonn. And I went for two years to Bonn. And there I published a very large documentary iconography on Alexander von Humboldt. In the meantime, I was doing still research and published that big, famous iconography of Jews in Polish art from 18th century till now, painted by artists-- most famous Polish artists, of whom none was Jewish. But this is the most important visual documentary on Jews in Poland as they were seen by their neighbors. And they were seen in a very good light, from 18th century till now. I was teaching. And I educated a lot of students, who are having quite a high position. Later on, as soon my son finished his graduate study, I decide to do what I want-- always wanted to do. And then I came to work on my diary and on my poems, and so forth. In the meantime, I also worked as a volunteer in-- for International Executive Service Corps. And I went to Africa twice-- once for three months to Zimbabwe, to organize sculptures and galleries of sculptures and worked with sculptures. And last time, it was last year, in Malawi, to organize and do the same things for the Malawian artists, painters mostly. This was the third continent I was in. Because in 1950, I went to Moscow with a group of representatives from all walks of life in Poland. And I was representing students who were not belonging to any organization, political or communistic or whatever. And they found only students like this in a conservative Krakow, and in Krakow, of course, at the Department of Art History and Philosophy, which was still not socialistic enough. So this is when I went to Moscow. And there, I met Ilya Ehrenburg, Khachaturian-- who sent me, later on, his works-- and Glinka and many other important people. I saw, from far away, Stalin. I saw even Mao Tse-tung, because at this time they loved one another. And here, of all my-- I also, of course, participated in many international conferences in Europe and here. And I think, for a woman who was a mother and single parent, so to speak, is quite an achievement-- all these books and all these publications. However, the most important achievement, it's my son and my grandson. Because I always felt like actually uprooted tree, with cut-off branches except for one. And now that branch has a little twig. And this is that little Jason. And always in my life it was most important, no matter what, that my son has a home and knows that this home is all. Because we have no other family except one another. And I think that, for me, coming from such a loving, warm home as I did, maybe this home and this family are responsible for my resistance, so to speak-- psychological, moral, whatever-- that I survived.

speaker: interviewer
text: And on a, on a, on a, on a similar tack, looking back over the whole experience and how you were able to survive, do you feel that, in the heart of all these atrocities, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] you were still able to feel God's presence [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] in the midst of it all?

speaker: interviewee
text: First of all, I was not even realizing whether I am religious or not, before the war. It was more tradition, but it was not so much in a spiritual or religious way. But I was always very sensitive to nature. And I remember that, when we were standing on those roll calls, particularly in Plaszow, which was on a hill over the city of Krakow, and this was very early in the morning. And there was sun-- sunrise. And my mother said, 'Look how beautiful, look at this. Don't think about anything terrible here, look at this beauty. We can look at this beauty of sunrise, while the Germans cannot, because they have to count us.' And I saw, somehow, maybe, that presence of God in the beauty of nature. And well, you probably noticed that in my house are plenty of flowers. For five years, I never had any flowers, because in camp, there was nothing. But there was the sky. And there, there were not even trees there. But there was the sky. In a way, I was really, in my-- in my mind-- not by prayer-- praying to God when I was running in Auschwitz, trying to find out my mother and bring her there. And it was-- we did not have time. But it was three different times when I saved the life of my mother. In a way, I saved the life of my sister-in-law, too. [SIGHS] Well, but then when I in my poems sometimes, I was quarreling with God. I have one poem when I said, even Christ was supposed to save all of us and mankind. But we have to save ourselves, by ourselves, not by other, higher things. We cannot count on it. However, when I was really at the end in Ravensbruck, then I prayed, 'Dear Lord, I cannot any longer. Please save me. Please.' And he did.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now there was a song that you wanted to sing. Was there not?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was a funny song. We were singing many songs. Everybody knew it. [SINGS IN POLISH] Well, this was song from ghetto, when we were coming for work. And somehow, every one of my songs is ending with somewhat optimistic, well. When we are coming in the-- where we are free, outside of ghetto, but in the evening we have to go back. But there will come time when the sun will shine for us, too.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you want to sing that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, this is this, what I was singing. [SINGS POLISH] Well, this was just the song-- well, we can just now curse our fate, but the sun will come. But at the end, when at-- of the war, when we were in Malchow, and it was Yalta and we knew that about two weeks, we will be free. There was something like, like magic. Two weeks, in two weeks, everything will be fine. So we were singing like this-- that hey, whoever is healthy, grab bread under your arm, and march to Krakow. [SINGS IN POLISH]

speaker: interviewer
text: Beautiful. Now, if there was a message that you could leave for your grandchild, or perhaps for the world--

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know. I, I never-- I hated to use big words, except if ironically. But, but this is something what I really wanted to say. And when my diary was published, this was the summary. This was my statement. 'The Legacy of the Holocaust. The invasion of Poland on September 1, 1939, began the Second World War, which ended May 9, 1945. In the meantime, there were 2,077 days, which had to be lived through. No one in the world was, or could have been, prepared for the art of existence and the art of dying under German occupation. For us, the known, familiar world turned into the darkest void, where ghettos emerged, labor camps, concentration and death camps, like isolated planets, united by the unknown-to-us common goal, extermination. The uprising in Warsaw ghetto, the heroic struggle, not for life but for death with dignity, was the proudest moment in the martyrology of Polish Jews. The uprising overshadowed somewhat another, very long struggle, equally heroic, albeit without arms and open battle. On those strange planets of our surrealistic existence went an everyday struggle for life, human life. According to individual cultural and religious tradition, it manifested itself in attempts to bring some order, compassion, some cultivation, and satisfying of needs for education, for the arts, poetry reading, and concerts. It was evident in organizing of clandestine schools, hospitals and orphanages. Not German did this, no, we. We ourselves. It manifested itself in voluntary care for the destitute, as long as it was officially permissible to be sick, old, or just a small child. Soon this became a crime perishable by death. Shortly after, the strong and healthy also went by millions to the gas chambers. During 1942-45, the years of Final Solution, the struggle still did not cease. It just took different forms. While recalling the horror of destruction, the counter-current of unselfish, instinctive solidarity of victimized people should never be forgotten. People were helping one another-- obviously, families, then members of the same community, city, or village. Later, as our world shrank, people from the same camp, the same barrack, bunk, just human to human, Jews or non-Jews belonging to different nations, speaking different languages. Confronted constantly with life-death situation, we had to respond at once, without maybe, perhaps, what should or could be done. To join the OD ghetto police for privileges, or to stay away from identifying oneself with oppressors. To drag one's feet at forced labor, while, therefore, others have to work twice as hard. To smuggle food under the jacket, although if caught, it meant shooting of the entire kommando, always punished collectively and always by death. And during endless roll calls, to protect a sick inmate from the hawk eye of inspecting SS officer by moving to the front row, while all we wished then was to be invisible. Still, this is how people saved me during typhoid fever in Ravensbruck. And during the deportation, to go to the right with Mother or to the left with a lover. This question did not exist for parents. I do not know one example of a parent escaping alone. But I know of many parents who could have been rescued, yet went to death with their children, small or grown, who could not. In this stark, black-and-white world without nuances, we faced decisions-- in fact, moral choices-- all the time. Although the circumstances might have excused negative behavior, I like to stress that the choices were mostly ethical. The inhuman environment intensified character traits. A bad person tended to become evil. A good one, almost a saint. Such people could be found in every group of five or 10 prisoners who choose the one just person to cut loaf of bread into even, daily portions. Such people mobilized others to be considerate in sharing. And people were helping others, if possible, by direct action; if not, by encouraging word, song, and poem, a friendly smile or a kind gesture, a reassuring glance-- all equally important for not giving up hope. Except for the fact that the war was finally won by the Allies, there is no rational explanation why and how anyone, Jews or non-Jews, survived Hitler's concentration camp. And we Jewish survivors of the Holocaust, because million perished and we did not, we have the awesome responsibility to speak for all of us, and to give the true testimony against the image often distorted by the shallow theories put forward by some expert on the Holocaust. Their socio-psychological deliberations and dehumanizing statistics tarnish the legacy of the 20th century Holocaust. I would like to recall each of us, one by one, as we were in our instinctive, constant struggle for our and others' humanity, physical and moral survival. Striving so hard to stand upright and live normal life in absurd circumstances on the edge of total destruction, we clung to the traditional values and strove to retain basic human decency.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what is the legacy of the Holocaust to me? To me, it is the prevalence of human integrity and goodness of heart, which is unbending, even in the evil and ocean of  hatred. This human integrity and goodness of heart gave me courage, hope, and strength to survive, and to live free of bitterness and hatred.' Halina, on behalf of Steven Spielberg's Survivors of the Shoah Visual History Foundation, we would like to thank you for so richly and passionately sharing your experiences with us this day. God bless you.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you. You, too.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you, thank you.

speaker: interviewer
text: Say it doesn't rain Halina, to yourself.

speaker: interviewee
text: This sofa is full of Nelkens family. Here is my daughter-in-law, Helen Ronan Nelken, an art historian. This is my son, Les Daniel Nelken, an architect and something new, now--

speaker: interviewee
text: Computer graphics.

speaker: interviewee
text: Computer graphics. And this is my little grandson, Jason Nelken. And here is [? Mother. ?] [CHUCKLING] See? OK, so--

speaker: interviewee
text: I guess, I guess I'm here because my mom survived. And I guess there's a lot of people that can't be being videotaped right now. Because that didn't happen. And just for future people that may be watching this, I hope that, if you can look around in the world and make sure that there aren't things going on, things like in Bosnia and places like Rwanda, because I think there's a lot of different forms of the Holocaust. And some of them can take place in-- with machetes in Rwanda and mortar shells in Bosnia. And please look around, if you're-- you know, look at your world and see if there's anything you can do to help those kind of people. Because it can happen any time, and I think it even has since.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I'm glad that Jason's here. I think he's the legacy, or the continuation of the Nelken family. And it's great that he can continue to have the name.

speaker: interviewee
text: And this is yesterday was Thanksgiving. And I was so grateful that they were able to produce such a beautiful little Nelkenik, yeah. And they are such good parents, really.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you. Thank you very much.

speaker: interviewee
text: We thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: You say something?

speaker: interviewee
text: Say bye.

speaker: interviewee
text: Bye. [CHUCKLING]

speaker: interviewee
text: I sit? Shall I stand?

speaker: interviewer
text: You're fine where you're standing. Microphone in place? Tape rolling. No, just do it to the side. That's-- right there, go ahead.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my picture taken two-- three years ago, here in Cambridge, Massachusetts, with flowers from my very own balcony.

speaker: interviewer
text: [WHISPERS] That picture of your mother.

speaker: interviewee
text: And, by the way, in back of that video, there was always seen that picture of my mother, taken about 1960.

speaker: interviewer
text: (WHISPERING) In what location?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, taken in 1959 in Poland, in Krakow. This is my father. Picture taken to that identification card, in 1940 or 1941, in Krakow. This is the last photograph of my father in his life.

speaker: interviewer
text: [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewee
text: Emanuel Nelken, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] picture taken in Krakow in 1940 or beginning of '49, for his identification card. [GERMAN]

speaker: interviewer
text: Go ahead.

speaker: interviewee
text: They are my aunts and my mother, all four sisters. It was taken-- I don't know when but certainly before second-- before second-- First World War. It is, from, from the left-- where is left? Here?

speaker: interviewer
text: That's right.

speaker: interviewee
text: From the left, it's Aunt Lola, Aunt Bronka, my mother, who was 17 at this time, and Aunt Hella. Aunt Hella was in Vienna, living in Vienna, Aunt Bronka was living in Prague, and Aunt Hella in Slovakia. None of them, except for my mother, survive, because they all were in Theresienstadt. I mean these two aunts, Bronka and Lola. And Aunt Hella die in Vienna.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Go ahead.

speaker: interviewee
text: They are my parents and my brother, at this time when I was not even planned. So probably, this is taken in about 1922 or 1923. And my father, Emanuel Nelken, is to the left, then is my brother Feliks. And then is my mother, Regina Nelken. Picture was taken in Krakow.

speaker: interviewer
text: Go ahead.

speaker: interviewee
text: This picture was taken two days before the outbreak of the war in Krakow, on the Royal Castle in Wawel. It's, of course, about the 10th of August, 1939. And I am in the middle, surrounded by my two friends, who sign to me, Halina surrounded by two dwarfs. Because this was the summer when I shoot up. So I grow up a lot, and you see how much taller I am than my friends. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] We deserve a big pizza.

speaker: interviewer
text: Go ahead, talk about it.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is already in ghetto, in 1941 in Krakow. I am to the left with my father and my friend, Staszka. And this--

speaker: interviewer
text: (WHISPERING) To your father's left.

speaker: interviewee
text: Here, yeah. And while we are all having those armbands with David's star-- my father, you can see, and my friend-- so that people should know that we are Jewish.

speaker: interviewer
text: Go ahead.

speaker: interviewee
text: 1941, Halina Nelken in Krakow, in ghetto.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell us.

speaker: interviewee
text: First page of my diary. It means 'Why?' Here I am stretching my arms to the world, and then I am in shade of the world. And it was in September 19--
